title: To What Extent Do Citrus Fruits Contribute to Enamel Demineralization?
abstract: This study investigates the erosive effect of common citrus fruits on tooth enamel by measuring surface hardness change after controlled acid exposure, comparing lemon, orange, and grapefruit juice against a water control.
researchQuestion: To what extent does exposure to citrus fruit juice contribute to enamel demineralization compared to a neutral control, and does this effect vary by fruit type?
methodology: Extracted bovine enamel samples were divided into four groups and immersed in lemon, orange, grapefruit juice, or distilled water for a fixed exposure period. Surface microhardness was measured before and after exposure using a Vickers hardness tester, and percentage change in hardness was compared across groups using one-way ANOVA.
findings: All three citrus juices produced statistically significant enamel softening relative to the water control (p < 0.05), with lemon juice producing the largest mean hardness loss, consistent with its lower pH. Orange juice produced the smallest effect among the three fruits tested.
significance: The results support existing dental health guidance to limit direct, prolonged citrus exposure to enamel and rinse with water afterward, and offer a simple comparative reference for relative erosive risk across common citrus fruits.

title: The Effect of Citrus Fruits on Human Enamel Demineralisation: An Investigation Using Pig Teeth as a Model
abstract: This study examines how citrus fruit acidity affects tooth enamel, using pig incisors as a model for human teeth. Ten teeth were soaked in lemon juice and ten in lime juice for seven days, and mass loss was measured as an indicator of demineralization. Lemon juice (pH 1.54) caused greater average mass loss (0.52 g) than lime juice (pH 1.90, 0.35 g), consistent with the hypothesis that lower pH drives more enamel corrosion. A t-test gave p = 0.238, showing the difference wasn't statistically significant at 95% confidence, though the trend matched predicted chemistry.
researchQuestion: To what extent do citrus fruits contribute to enamel demineralization of pig teeth?
methodology: 20 pig incisors (10 per juice type) were sterilized, weighed, and submerged in fresh lemon or lime juice for 7 days under controlled temperature and volume conditions. Mass and pH were recorded before and after, then compared using mean mass loss and a two-sample t-test.
findings: All 20 teeth lost mass, confirming demineralization in both groups. Lemon-soaked teeth lost more mass (0.52 g) than lime-soaked teeth (0.35 g), matching lemon's lower pH and higher H+ concentration. The difference was not statistically significant (p = 0.238), so the null hypothesis wasn't rejected, though results still followed the expected chemical trend despite limited statistical strength.
significance: The findings link everyday diet to measurable dental effects, showing that acidic fruits can erode enamel with prolonged exposure — reinforcing the value of rinsing after acidic food and connecting basic acid-base chemistry to real biological outcomes.

title: Effect of Temperature on the Hydrolysis Rate of Aspirin
abstract: This study examined how temperature affects the rate at which aspirin breaks down in an acidic solution. pH was measured over time at different temperatures, showing that higher temperatures caused faster hydrolysis and a more rapid decrease in pH.
researchQuestion: How does temperature affect the rate of aspirin hydrolysis in an acidic solution?
methodology: Aspirin was mixed with hydrochloric acid, and the solution was heated to temperatures between 30°C and 70°C. pH changes were monitored over time using a calibrated pH probe, with each temperature tested five times.
findings: Higher temperatures increased the rate of aspirin hydrolysis, with the fastest reaction occurring at 70°C, consistent with the expected relationship between temperature and reaction rate.
significance: The study demonstrates how temperature influences chemical reaction rates and highlights its importance in pharmaceutical stability and reaction kinetics.

title: Effect of Ethanol Concentration on Beetroot Cell Membrane Permeability
abstract: This study investigated how different concentrations of ethanol affect the permeability of beetroot cell membranes. Membrane damage was measured by the amount of red pigment released from the cells, using a colorimeter. Results showed that higher ethanol concentrations caused greater pigment leakage, indicating increased membrane permeability.
researchQuestion: How does ethanol concentration affect the permeability of beetroot cell membranes?
methodology: Beetroot cylinders were placed in ethanol solutions ranging from 20% to 100%. After exposure, the absorbance of the surrounding solution was measured with a colorimeter, with each concentration tested five times under controlled conditions.
findings: Higher ethanol concentrations caused greater pigment leakage, showing that increasing ethanol concentration increases cell membrane permeability.
significance: The study demonstrates how ethanol can damage cell membranes and provides insight into membrane structure and the effects of chemical substances on living cells.

title: Government Intervention in Modern Economies: Price Floors, Monetary Policy, and Trade Tariffs
abstract: A collection of three short economic commentaries examining real-world government interventions: a minimum price floor on alcohol in Scotland, an interest rate cut by India's central bank, and US tariffs on Indian exports. Each case is analyzed using standard microeconomic and macroeconomic models to assess effects on price, quantity, market efficiency, and economic well-being.
researchQuestion: How do different forms of government intervention — price floors, monetary policy, and trade tariffs — affect market outcomes, efficiency, and economic well-being?
methodology: Each commentary applies relevant economic models (supply and demand, externality analysis, AD-AS diagrams, and tariff-incidence diagrams) to a real news article covering a government intervention, evaluating the likely effects on consumers, producers, and the broader economy.
findings: A minimum price floor on alcohol reduced consumption only modestly due to inelastic demand, despite an associated fall in alcohol-related deaths. An interest rate cut in India was found likely to stimulate aggregate demand and improve economic well-being, particularly for lower-income households, though subject to policy lags. US tariffs on Indian exports were projected to significantly reduce export revenue and create a recessionary gap, though India's largely domestic-demand economy was found to offer some cushioning effect.
significance: Together, the three commentaries show how foundational economic models — price floors, AD-AS analysis, and tariff incidence — can be applied to interpret current, real-world policy decisions and their effects on markets and welfare.
